description: A DID listed in `audience[]` of a `private` context MUST NOT find that context via GET /contexts/search. Search visibility for `private` is strictly agent_id-only, regardless of audience membership — the `audience` field grants retrieval access only (vis-004), not discovery. RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.5 and RFC-ACDP-0005 §2.5.5 (Q1) define the asymmetry.

scenarios.name: Producer (agent_id) searches and sees both contexts
Audience member searches: sees ONLY the public context
scenarios.notes: Audience members CAN retrieve the private context if they know the ctx_id (vis-004) but MUST NOT find it via search. total_estimate MUST also be 1 — including the private context in the count for an audience member leaks its existence to a requester who can guess at activity by watching the count.

scenarios.name: Audience member searches with derived_from filter pointing at the private context
scenarios.notes: The `derived_from` filter is search (RFC-ACDP-0005 §2.4), not retrieval. Even an audience member of a private context cannot use it to enumerate descendants. Note: in this scenario the search target is the private context's ctx_id, exposed via the URL parameter; the response MUST behave identically to any other search filter that would otherwise surface a private context to a non-agent_id requester.

scenarios.name: Outsider searches: sees ONLY the public context
scenarios.notes: Outsider sees the same scoped result as the audience member, because the search visibility for private is strictly agent_id-only. This is the joint-equivalence (RFC-ACDP-0005 §2.5.5) — search results, total_estimate, and cursor pages all converge to the same scoping.
